title: Arroz caldoso de marisco
subtitle: Soupy Spanish seafood rice with saffron and tomato
excerpt: A soupy rice with king prawns, mussels, and squid in a saffron-scented fish stock, with a base of onion, garlic, and tomato. Looser than paella and eaten with a spoon, the rice absorbs the seafood broth as it cooks and the result is halfway between a risotto and a soup.
sourceNotes: Arroz caldoso (literally 'brothy rice') is a distinct Spanish rice preparation, separate from paella, in which the rice-to-liquid ratio is much higher and the finished dish is served semi-liquid. It is particularly associated with Valencia, the Valencian coast, and the Levant, and is common across Spain's coastal regions wherever good fish stock is available. The dish predates paella as a category and represents the everyday rice cooking of Spanish fishing communities.

content.text: The stock is the foundation. Fish stock made with prawn shells and fish bones is the ideal, if you are using bought stock, supplement it with the shells from the prawns steeped in hot water for 10 minutes before straining. The saffron must be steeped in warm water for at least 10 minutes before it goes in; cold water barely extracts its colour.

Arroz caldoso uses a higher liquid ratio than paella: about 4 parts stock to 1 part rice. The rice cooks and swells in the broth, releasing starch that thickens the surrounding liquid slightly. The result is spoonable, not scoopable, if it sets firm, it is paella, not arroz caldoso. Serve immediately: the rice continues to absorb liquid and will thicken further as it sits.

items.name: Saffron
items.prepNote: steeped in 3 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes

content.text: Build the base and toast the rice

Heat the {{olive-oil}} in a wide, deep casserole over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} and {{squid}} rings and cook for 2 minutes. Stir in the {{tomato-puree}} and {{paprika-smoked}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{paella-rice}} and stir for 1 minute to coat the grains in the sofrito.

Pour in the hot {{stock-fish}} and add the {{saffron}} with its soaking water. Season with salt and bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer. Cook uncovered for 12 minutes, stirring occasionally. Add the {{mussels}} and {{king-prawns}}, pushing them into the rice. Cover and cook for a further 5 minutes until the mussels have opened and the prawns are pink and cooked through. Discard any mussels that haven't opened. Rest for 5 minutes. Scatter {{parsley-flat}} over and serve with {{lemon}} wedges.

items.fix: Add another ladle of hot stock and stir it through. Arroz caldoso should be brothy. Serve at once.
items.cause: Too little liquid or cooked too long uncovered
items.symptom: Rice has absorbed all the liquid and set firm like paella

items.fix: Paella-style rice absorbs quickly, check at 12 minutes and adjust; the rice continues cooking off the heat
items.cause: Simmered too long or at too high a heat

items.fix: Discard any that were open raw and did not close when tapped; give closed cooked ones another 2 minutes, if still shut, discard
items.cause: Insufficient heat or shells were closed dead before cooking
items.symptom: Mussels have not opened after 5 minutes

items.fix: Cook the sofrito for the full 8 minutes before adding rice; use a good fish stock or steep prawn shells in hot water for 10 minutes and strain
items.cause: Stock was weak or the sofrito underdeveloped
items.symptom: Broth is thin and lacks depth

content.text: Arroz caldoso is the rice dish that Valencia eats at home when it is not making paella for visitors. It requires neither a wide, flat pan nor the careful, non-stirring technique of paella (it is a casserole dish, forgiving and direct. The soupy texture makes it a winter rice, eaten when the weather is cold enough to want broth. Along the Valencian coast and in the fishing villages of Murcia and Alicante, it is made with whatever the boats brought in that morning) prawns, clams, cuttlefish, and the broth is made from the carcasses and shells, which is the reason it tastes the way it does.
